name: Orestes
description: Orestes is a central figure in Greek mythology, primarily known for his role in avenging the murder of his father, Agamemnon, by his mother, Clytemnestra, and her lover, Aegisthus. The son of Agamemnon and Clytemnestra, and brother to Electra, Iphigenia, and Chrysothemis, Orestes was away from Mycenae when Agamemnon returned from the Trojan War and was subsequently killed. Years later, spurred by his sister Electra and compelled by Apollo's oracle at Delphi, Orestes returned to Mycenae to exact revenge. He killed both Clytemnestra and Aegisthus, an act that, while fulfilling divine justice, also brought upon him the wrath of the Erinyes (Furies), goddesses of vengeance. Orestes was then pursued by the Furies, driven mad by his matricide. He sought purification and absolution, eventually finding it through Apollo's intervention and Athena's judgment in Athens, where he was acquitted, ending the cycle of violence within the House of Atreus. His story explores themes of justice, revenge, duty, and the transition from blood feuds to a more formalized legal system. Orestes's tale remains a powerful exploration of moral complexities and the consequences of violence, deeply impacting Western literature and thought. His trials highlight the conflict between divine commands and human morality, ultimately leading to a resolution that emphasizes forgiveness and the rule of law.
subtitle: Avenger of Agamemnon, pursued by Furies
achievements: Overcame madness induced by the Furies
Brought an end to the curse on the House of Atreus

keyMyths.title: The Murder of Agamemnon
keyMyths.description: Agamemnon, returning from the Trojan War, is murdered by his wife Clytemnestra and her lover Aegisthus. This act sets the stage for Orestes's eventual revenge and the cycle of violence.

keyMyths.title: The Oracle at Delphi
keyMyths.description: Apollo's oracle commands Orestes to avenge his father's death by killing Clytemnestra and Aegisthus. This divine mandate places Orestes in a moral dilemma, forcing him to choose between filial duty and the crime of matricide.

keyMyths.title: The Trial in Athens
keyMyths.description: Pursued by the Furies after committing matricide, Orestes seeks purification and is eventually tried in Athens. Athena casts the deciding vote, acquitting Orestes and ending the cycle of vengeance.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: A trilogy of plays (Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, The Eumenides) that detail the murder of Agamemnon, Orestes's revenge, and his trial.
sources.text: A trilogy of plays (Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, The Eumenides) that detail the murder of Agamemnon, Orestes's revenge, and his trial.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A play focusing on Electra's role in inciting Orestes to avenge their father's death.
sources.text: A play focusing on Electra's role in inciting Orestes to avenge their father's death.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: Another version of the Electra story, offering a different perspective on the events leading to Orestes's revenge.
sources.text: Another version of the Electra story, offering a different perspective on the events leading to Orestes's revenge.
